old_claim.note: Anatomy (Early Studies) is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency is retained because Hippocratic clinical observation and prognosis predate source-backed Alexandrian human dissection.

source_shape.source_locator: Life and works section describes Hippocratic Epidemics as annual records, case histories, treatments, diagnosis, and prognosis, and notes no systematic human dissection in Hippocrates' time.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Britannica describes Hippocratic clinical medicine as observation, case histories, diagnosis, and prognosis without systematic human dissection.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source supports clinical observation as an empirical Hippocratic practice that did not depend on the later Alexandrian anatomy node.

ontology_before: Clinical observation was modeled as depending on early anatomy studies, creating a false later prerequisite for Hippocratic diagnostic practice.
ontology_after: Clinical observation remains connected to writing, record keeping, and humoral theory, while anatomy is separated as a later medical-science development.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant changed: Hippocratic clinical observation must remain independent of the later Alexandrian anatomy node.
would_reject_if: A source shows that this node is meant only to cover post-Alexandrian anatomical diagnosis.
The anatomy node is rescoped to non-dissective observational anatomy before Hippocratic medicine.
A source demonstrates that Hippocratic diagnosis required systematic human dissection.
short_reason: Hippocratic clinical observation predates and does not require Alexandrian anatomical dissection.
